_meta.note: Regression reference for MeetingBSDetector. Re-run each case's input against the live backend (npm run check:golden meeting-bs-detector) and diff for QUALITY not exact match: all sections present, non-empty arrays stay non-empty, no truncation, numbers consistent (time_cost.total_person_hours == duration*participants). The calendar + report cases GUARD the success-guard fix: both MUST return 200 (they 500'd before — guards checked parsed.verdict but schemas emit week_verdict / grade). See audit/MEETINGBSDETECTOR-NOTES.md.

input.meetingText: Weekly engineering all-hands, standing recurring, 22 people, no agenda, status updates

output.one_liner: 22 people paying 22 person-hours weekly to hear status updates they could read in five minutes.
reasoning: No agenda means attendees arrive with no context, no preparation, and no shared definition of what 'done' looks like — the meeting cannot succeed by design.
Status updates are pull information: they should live in a shared doc or async tool so people read only what's relevant to them, not broadcast to 22 people simultaneously.
22 attendees obliterates any real discussion — at one hour, each person gets under 3 minutes of airtime if time were distributed equally, which it never is.
Recurring with no stated deliverables is a textbook entropy trap: the meeting persists because it always has, not because it produces anything.
The sole green flag — one hour, not two — is the only thing keeping this from an outright cancel verdict; there's a salvageable core if the format is restructured.
red_flags: 22 attendees — more than double the 10-person red flag threshold
Recurring standing meeting with no stated deliverables
Purpose is status updates — a classic async-replaceable format
green_flags: Engineering all-hands can have legitimate culture and alignment value if structured correctly

time_cost.annual_cost_if_recurring: If weekly, this meeting consumes 1,144 person-hours per year — the equivalent of roughly 28.5 full work weeks of a single engineer's time.

output.alternative: Replace with a shared async status doc updated Friday by each team lead; hold a 15-minute live sync monthly for genuine cross-team blockers and culture moments only.
rescue_tips: Publish a capped agenda 24 hours before: max 3 items, each with an owner and a time box — no agenda, no meeting.
Split the format: 10 minutes of pre-read async updates (written, not spoken), 20 minutes live for decisions and blockers only, then hard stop at 30 minutes.
Audit attendance ruthlessly — identify the 6-8 people who actually need to hear decisions in real time and make everyone else optional with a shared summary doc sent within the hour.
output.decline_message: Thanks for including me — I want to make sure my time in this meeting is actually useful. Since the format is status updates without an agenda, I'm going to opt out this week and catch up via the shared notes afterward. If there's a decision or blocker where you need my input directly, flag me and I'll join or respond async same day.
output.optimal_format: 10-min async written status thread (Slack or Notion) + optional 20-min monthly live sync for decisions and blockers only, capped at 8 people

output.one_liner: The standup is quietly eating 22.5 person-hours a week on status updates that a shared doc could replace in minutes.

meetings.title: Daily Standup — Status Report
meetings.reason: 30 minutes for 9 people is a team meeting disguised as a standup — true standups run 10 minutes max with a strict 3-question format, saving 22.5 person-hours per week vs. the current 22.5 burned.

meetings.title: Sprint Planning — Weekly
meetings.reason: 2 hours of collaborative planning for 8 people is well within acceptable range for a recurring planning session that directly produces work alignment and sprint commitments.

meetings.title: 1:1 — Manager Check-in
meetings.reason: 30 minutes between 2 people is one of the highest-ROI meeting formats that exists — coaching, unblocking, and trust-building cannot be replicated async.

output.weekly_advice: Cut the standup to 10 minutes with a hard timer and the three classic questions only — anything that needs discussion gets a parking lot and a separate 15-minute huddle with only the relevant people.
output.meeting_free_blocks: Protect mornings after the standup (roughly 9:10–12:00) and all afternoons as deep-work blocks — sprint planning is your one justified afternoon anchor.

output.period: Based on 12 meetings analyzed with 2 detailed scorecards submitted.
output.headline_stat: 15 person-hours per week lost to meetings — that's nearly half a standard workweek before you've done any real work.

key_stats.label: Weekly meeting load
key_stats.value: 15 person-hours per week sitting in meetings

key_stats.label: Person-hours already rescued
key_stats.value: 18 person-hours saved through smarter meeting decisions

key_stats.label: Decisions made in scored meetings
key_stats.value: 0 out of 2 scored meetings produced an actual decision

key_stats.label: Right people in the room
key_stats.value: 0 out of 2 scored meetings had only the necessary attendees

key_stats.label: Meetings that could have been shorter
key_stats.value: Both scored meetings ran longer than the content required

key_stats.label: Meetings analyzed in total
key_stats.value: 12 meetings reviewed — solid dataset to spot patterns

output.biggest_win: 18 person-hours already reclaimed — proof that meeting awareness is actively working and the habit is sticking. 💪
output.biggest_problem: Zero scored meetings produced a decision AND had the right people — your meetings are gatherings, not engines of progress.

time_analysis.maker_time_ratio: For every 1 hour in a meeting, you need to protect at least 2 hours of uninterrupted focus time — right now that ratio is dangerously flipped.

recommendations: 🎯 No agenda, no meeting — every invite must answer 'what decision or output does this produce?' before it gets calendar space.
✂️ Cut the default duration: move 60-minute meetings to 45, 30-minute meetings to 20 — time expands to fill the slot every single time.
👤 Halve the invite list — for each scored meeting, every attendee who didn't need to be there cost person-hours you can't get back; make attendance opt-in with a shared doc instead.
📋 End every meeting with a 60-second decision log read aloud — if you can't name the decision made, the meeting shouldn't have happened.
output.share_summary: Our team spends 15 person-hours a week in meetings — and scored meetings are producing zero decisions with the wrong people in the room. 🚨 The good news: we've already reclaimed 18 person-hours by paying attention, and that number is going up.
